## 🤖 Claude (Orchestrator)

You are picking up session 026 of the tcc-darwin8-ppc project — a Tiny C Compiler backend for PowerPC Mac OS X 10.4 Tiger. You have no memory of prior conversations; everything you need is in this prompt and the repo.

## The goal

After session 025, plain `tcc -o exe file.c` produces working libSystem-using executables on Tiger PPC, no gcc involvement. BUT `tcc-self` (tcc compiling itself) still needs `gcc-4.0` for the link step, because `tcc.c` references libgcc runtime helpers that aren't in our `libtcc1.a`. Specifically (from a recent dyld error on the missing-symbol list):

- `___ashldi3`
- `___fixdfdi`
- `___fixunsdfdi`
- `___lshrdi3`
- `___udivdi3`
- `___umoddi3`

(`___floatundidf` is already provided by `tcc/lib/lib-ppc.c`.)

Your job: get these helpers into `libtcc1.a` so `tcc -o tcc-self tcc.o libtcc1.a` works **without** `/usr/lib/gcc/.../libgcc.a`.

## The implementation choice

`tcc/lib/libtcc1.c` already contains portable C implementations of all these helpers (search for `__udivdi3`, `__ashldi3`, etc.). They're gated behind `#if !defined __x86_64__ && !defined __arm__ && !defined __riscv && !defined __aarch64__` — i.e. enabled for i386, disabled for the modern targets that have native 64-bit ops or compiler builtins. PPC32 needs them because it has no native 64-bit divide.

The `__udivmoddi4` implementation in libtcc1.c uses four architecture-specific macros (`sub_ddmmss`, `umul_ppmm`, `udiv_qrnnd`, `count_leading_zeros`). Currently only i386 inline-asm versions exist. You have two paths:

**Path A (preferred):** Add a PPC branch that defines these macros in portable C. Sketches:

```c
#define sub_ddmmss(sh, sl, ah, al, bh, bl) \
  do { USItype __x = (al) - (bl); \
       (sh) = (ah) - (bh) - ((al) < (bl)); \
       (sl) = __x; } while (0)

#define umul_ppmm(w1, w0, u, v) \
  do { USItype __ul = (USItype)(u) & 0xffff, __uh = (USItype)(u) >> 16; \
       USItype __vl = (USItype)(v) & 0xffff, __vh = (USItype)(v) >> 16; \
       USItype __x0 = __ul * __vl, __x1 = __ul * __vh; \
       USItype __x2 = __uh * __vl, __x3 = __uh * __vh; \
       __x1 += (__x0 >> 16); __x1 += __x2; \
       if (__x1 < __x2) __x3 += 0x10000; \
       (w1) = __x3 + (__x1 >> 16); \
       (w0) = (__x1 << 16) | (__x0 & 0xffff); } while (0)
```

**Critical:** do NOT implement `umul_ppmm` as `(uint64_t)u * v` — that would call `__muldi3` recursively when compiled by tcc. Use the 16x16 split trick above.

For `count_leading_zeros`, a portable implementation:
```c
#define count_leading_zeros(c, x) do { \
    USItype __x = (x), __c = 0; \
    if (__x == 0) { (c) = 32; break; } \
    if (!(__x & 0xffff0000)) { __c += 16; __x <<= 16; } \
    if (!(__x & 0xff000000)) { __c +=  8; __x <<=  8; } \
    if (!(__x & 0xf0000000)) { __c +=  4; __x <<=  4; } \
    if (!(__x & 0xc0000000)) { __c +=  2; __x <<=  2; } \
    if (!(__x & 0x80000000)) { __c +=  1; } \
    (c) = __c; } while (0)
```

For `udiv_qrnnd(q, r, n1, n0, d)` — 64-by-32 divide producing 32-bit quotient + remainder — the simplest approach is a 32-iteration shift-subtract loop. Look up "udiv_qrnnd portable C" or write one yourself; it's ~10 lines.

**Path B (fallback if Path A doesn't compile via tcc-on-PPC):** Write a separate `tcc/lib/lib-ppc-libgcc.c` with self-contained C implementations of just the six helpers we need, using simple shift-subtract for divide. Slower but guaranteed to work. Add it to the OBJ list. This is more code but skips the libtcc1.c integration entirely.

Try Path A first. If it hits issues you can't quickly resolve, fall back to Path B and document why.

## Files to modify

- `tcc/lib/libtcc1.c` — open the architecture gate for PPC (the `#if !defined __x86_64__ && !defined __arm__ && !defined __riscv && !defined __aarch64__` at ~line 110), add PPC macro definitions. Look for the existing i386 block under `#if defined __i386__` for the structural template.
- `tcc/lib/Makefile` — add `libtcc1.o` to `OBJ-ppc-osx`. Currently it's just `lib-ppc.o $(OSX_O)`.
- `scripts/bootstrap-tcc-self.sh` — currently uses `gcc-4.0` to link tcc-self. Replace that step with a tcc-self-link path: `./tcc/tcc -B./tcc -I./tcc -o "$OUT" "$WORK/tcc.o" ./tcc/libtcc1.a`. Drop the libgcc.a reference, the `-arch ppc` flag, the `gcc-4.0` invocation. Keep the same `OUT` path.

## How to build and test

The PPC build host is `ibookg37` (ssh works passwordless from this Mac). The repo lives at `~/tcc-darwin8-ppc/` on ibookg37 — same paths as the local checkout. Sync changes via:

```sh
rsync -a --delete --exclude='.git' --exclude='tcc/config.h' --exclude='tcc/config.mak' --exclude='tcc/Makefile' --exclude='tcc/*.o' --exclude='tcc/*.a' --exclude='tcc/tcc' --exclude='tcc/tcc-doc.html' /Users/cell/claude/tcc-darwin8-ppc/ ibookg37:tcc-darwin8-ppc/
```

Then on ibookg37:

```sh
ssh ibookg37 'cd ~/tcc-darwin8-ppc && ./scripts/build-tiger.sh 2>&1 | tail -10'
```

(The build script handles configure + make.)

## Test sequence (each must pass before declaring success)

```sh
# 1. tcc compiles tcc.c (sanity)
ssh ibookg37 'cd ~/tcc-darwin8-ppc && rm -f /tmp/tcc.o && ./tcc/tcc -c ./tcc/tcc.c -o /tmp/tcc.o -B./tcc -I./tcc 2>&1 | tail -3'

# 2. tcc links tcc.o + libtcc1.a (NO libgcc.a) — this is the new capability
ssh ibookg37 'cd ~/tcc-darwin8-ppc && ./tcc/tcc -B./tcc -I./tcc -o /tmp/tcc-self2 /tmp/tcc.o ./tcc/libtcc1.a 2>&1 | tail -3 && ls -la /tmp/tcc-self2'

# 3. tcc-self2 runs and prints version
ssh ibookg37 '/tmp/tcc-self2 -v'

# 4. Self-host fixpoint: tcc-self2 compiles tcc.c twice, output is byte-identical
ssh ibookg37 'cd ~/tcc-darwin8-ppc && rm -f /tmp/tcc.o.gen1 /tmp/tcc.o.gen2 && /tmp/tcc-self2 -c tcc/tcc.c -o /tmp/tcc.o.gen1 -B./tcc -I./tcc 2>&1 | tail -3 && /tmp/tcc-self2 -c tcc/tcc.c -o /tmp/tcc.o.gen2 -B./tcc -I./tcc 2>&1 | tail -3 && cmp /tmp/tcc.o.gen1 /tmp/tcc.o.gen2 && echo "FIXPOINT OK"'

# 5. The updated bootstrap script works end-to-end
ssh ibookg37 'cd ~/tcc-darwin8-ppc && ./scripts/bootstrap-tcc-self.sh 2>&1 | tail -5 && /tmp/tcc-self -v'

# 6. Existing demos still work (regression sweep)
ssh ibookg37 'cd ~/tcc-darwin8-ppc && ./demos/s025-self-link.sh 2>&1 | tail -5'
```

If any of these fail, debug and fix before declaring done.

Important: the `union double_long` has `lower` first, `upper` second — that's little-endian layout. PPC is big-endian, so `upper` comes first in memory. The `EXPD(fp)` macro uses `fp.l.upper` and `SIGND(fp)` uses `fp.l.upper`. With the current struct layout (lower=0, upper=1), `fp.l.upper` accesses the second 32-bit word, but on big-endian PPC the high word is the first word in memory.

The `__fixunsdfdi` function uses `EXPD(dl1)` which accesses `dl1.l.upper` (the field named "upper"). The struct has `lower` as first member and `upper` as second — on little-endian this means `upper` holds the high bits of the double. But on big-endian PPC, the high word is at the lower address, so the struct layout should have the high word first.

However, the `__fixunsdfdi` and `__fixdfdi` functions are already OUTSIDE the `#if !defined __x86_64__...` gate (they're at lines ~563-595), and they're outside the `#if !defined __arm__` guard too. Interesting! The big gate ends at line 481 with `#endif /* !__x86_64__ */`. So `__ashldi3`, `__lshrdi3`, etc. are inside `#if !defined __x86_64__ && !defined __arm__ && !defined __riscv && !defined __aarch64__` which ends at 481. 

The `__fixunsdfdi`, `__fixdfdi` are OUTSIDE this gate (lines 563-595), compiling for all architectures. The `__floatundidf` is at line 499, also outside the gate.

So the helpers I need to get working for PPC are:
1. `__ashldi3` - inside the gate (line ~463), need to open gate for PPC
2. `__lshrdi3` - inside the gate (line ~444), need to open gate for PPC
3. `__udivdi3` - inside the gate (line ~411), depends on `__udivmoddi4` which depends on the macros
4. `__umoddi3` - inside the gate (line ~416), same
5. `__fixdfdi` - already outside gate at line ~590, should compile for PPC
6. `__fixunsdfdi` - already outside gate at line ~563, should compile for PPC

But for `__udivmoddi4` (and thus `__udivdi3` and `__umoddi3`), I need to add PPC macro implementations of `sub_ddmmss`, `umul_ppmm`, `udiv_qrnnd`, and `count_leading_zeros`.

Also I need to check the `DWstruct` and big-endian issue. The `DWstruct` has `low, high` — on little-endian, `low` is the first (lower-address) word. On big-endian PPC, the low word is at the higher address. Let me check:

## 🤖 Claude (Subagent)

The `DWstruct` has `low` first, `high` second. For the union `DWunion.ll` to work correctly:
- On little-endian: `low` is at lower address = low 32 bits of the `long long`. Correct.
- On big-endian PPC: `low` is at lower address = HIGH 32 bits of the `long long`. Wrong!

This is a known issue with this code on big-endian. Let me look at the `__ashldi3` implementation more carefully:

Looking at `__ashldi3` (lines 463-479): it uses `u.s.high` and `u.s.low`. If these are swapped on PPC, the shift would be wrong.

But wait — these functions are compiled by tcc targeting PPC. TCC's code generator is the one producing the struct member access. TCC stores `long long` in memory as big-endian on PPC: the high word at lower address. And `struct DWstruct { Wtype low, high; }` — `low` maps to offset 0 (lower address = high bits on PPC).

So actually `u.s.low` in the DWunion would access the HIGH 32 bits of the long long on PPC, and `u.s.high` would access the LOW 32 bits. That means the bit-shift logic in `__ashldi3` would be inverted.

This is a real problem. Let me look at how the existing code in lib-ppc.c handles this. The `__floatundidf` in lib-ppc.c splits as `x >> 32` for high and `(unsigned int)x` for low — it doesn't use the DWunion struct at all, precisely to avoid this issue.

Let me think about this more carefully. On PPC (big-endian), `long long` is stored as: `[high32][low32]` in memory (high at lower address). A `struct { int low; int high; }` stored in memory is: `[low][high]` (low at lower address). So when overlapping via union:
- `u.ll` treated as big-endian: memory = `[highWord][lowWord]`
- `u.s.low` at offset 0 = same byte as high word of ll

So `u.s.low` actually contains the HIGH 32 bits and `u.s.high` contains the LOW 32 bits — names are reversed relative to their actual roles!

This would break `__ashldi3`, `__lshrdi3`, `__ashrdi3`. However, these functions use `#ifdef __TINYC__` guards — the non-tinyc path just uses `a << b` directly (which the compiler handles). So when compiled by tcc itself (which defines `__TINYC__`), these helpers would use the struct path.

Wait, but this code IS being compiled by tcc (since we're building libtcc1.a via tcc itself). So the `__TINYC__` path runs. And on PPC big-endian, the struct member naming is backwards.

Actually, hold on. Let me reconsider. The `DWunion` union is a C union, and whether the names are "correct" depends purely on whether the semantics match. Let me trace through `__ashldi3` with a big-endian PPC perspective:

```c
long long __ashldi3(long long a, int b) {
    DWunion u;
    u.ll = a;           // a stored big-endian: [a_high32][a_low32]
    // u.s.low (offset 0) = a_high32
    // u.s.high (offset 4) = a_low32
    if (b >= 32) {
        u.s.high = (unsigned)u.s.low << (b - 32);  // u.s.high(=a_low32) = a_high32 << (b-32) ... WRONG
        u.s.low = 0;
    } ...
}
```

Yes, this would be wrong. The correct behavior for `a << b` where `b >= 32` should be: result_high = a_low32 << (b-32), result_low = 0. But with swapped names: `u.s.high` (which is the low word on disk) gets set to `u.s.low` (which is the high word) shifted — that's wrong.

So the `__TINYC__` path in the shift helpers is broken for PPC big-endian. But the non-`__TINYC__` path (`return a << b`) is fine if the compiler supports 64-bit shifts natively (gcc-4.0 does). Since libtcc1.a is built by tcc (which defines `__TINYC__`), we have a problem.

But wait! Let me re-read. When tcc-on-PPC compiles `a << b` where a is `long long` — does tcc call `__ashldi3` recursively? If tcc emits a call to `__ashldi3` for 64-bit shifts, and `__ashldi3` itself has the broken `__TINYC__` path... that's a circular disaster.

The task statement says `___ashldi3` is one of the missing symbols. That means tcc.c's compilation emits calls to `__ashldi3`. And when building libtcc1.c, tcc would also need to handle 64-bit shifts... but since `__ashldi3` is being defined in that file, it presumably doesn't call itself.

Actually, when tcc compiles `__ashldi3` inside libtcc1.c, tcc uses the `#ifdef __TINYC__` path, which explicitly does the shift using 32-bit operations on `u.s.low` and `u.s.high` — no 64-bit shift at the C level. So it won't recurse.

The question is whether the logic is correct. Let me think again about what "correct" means for tcc on PPC.

In TCC's PPC backend, how is `long long` laid out? Let me check:

## 🤖 Claude (Subagent)

This confirms: TCC on PPC stores `long long` big-endian: HIGH word at lower address (offset), LOW word at offset+4. So for `DWunion`:
- `u.ll = a` stores: `[a_high32][a_low32]` in memory
- `u.s` has `struct { Wtype low, high; }`: `low` at offset 0, `high` at offset 4
- Therefore `u.s.low` = a_high32, `u.s.high` = a_low32

The struct member names are **backwards** relative to their semantic content on big-endian PPC. This IS a problem.

Let me look at `__ashldi3` again with this in mind:

```c
long long __ashldi3(long long a, int b) {
    DWunion u;
    u.ll = a;
    // u.s.low = a_high, u.s.high = a_low  (PPC big-endian)
    if (b >= 32) {
        u.s.high = (unsigned)u.s.low << (b - 32);  // a_low = a_high << (b-32) -- WRONG
        u.s.low = 0;                                 // a_high = 0
    } else if (b != 0) {
        u.s.high = ((unsigned)u.s.high << b) | ((unsigned)u.s.low >> (32 - b));  // a_low = ...
        u.s.low = (unsigned)u.s.low << b;  // a_high = ...
    }
    return u.ll;
}
```

This is inverted for PPC! The correct `a << b` for b >= 32 should be: result_high = a_low << (b-32), result_low = 0. But the code sets `u.s.low` (=a_high location) = 0 and `u.s.high` (=a_low location) = old `u.s.low` shifted. That's correct! Let me re-trace:

For `a << b` where b >= 32:
- Result HIGH should be: (a_low << (b-32))
- Result LOW should be: 0

With PPC naming (u.s.low = high position, u.s.high = low position):
- Code sets: `u.s.high` (=low position) = `(unsigned)u.s.low` (=old high) << (b-32) -- sets low=old_high<<(b-32), but should be old_low<<(b-32). WRONG.
- Code sets: `u.s.low` (=high position) = 0 -- sets high=0. CORRECT.

## 🤖 Claude (Subagent)

Yes, tcc defines `__TINYC__`. So when tcc on PPC compiles libtcc1.c, the `#ifdef __TINYC__` branches in `__ashldi3` etc. will be active, and they'll use the DWunion struct manipulation.

But actually — let me reconsider. The `__ashldi3`, `__lshrdi3` functions with the `#ifdef __TINYC__` path may actually work even with the swapped naming, because the logic might be written to work correctly regardless. Let me very carefully trace through `__ashldi3` for a specific case:

Consider `a = 0x0000000100000000LL` (value = 2^32), `b = 1`:
- On PPC, stored in memory as: `[0x00000001][0x00000000]`
- `u.s.low` (offset 0) = 0x00000001 (this is the HIGH word)
- `u.s.high` (offset 4) = 0x00000000 (this is the LOW word)

Code for b < 32:
```c
u.s.high = ((unsigned)u.s.high << b) | ((unsigned)u.s.low >> (32 - b));
u.s.low = (unsigned)u.s.low << b;
```
- `u.s.high` = (0x00000000 << 1) | (0x00000001 >> 31) = 0 | 0 = 0x00000000
- `u.s.low` = 0x00000001 << 1 = 0x00000002

Result: `u.s.low`=0x00000002 (at offset 0 = HIGH position), `u.s.high`=0x00000000 (at offset 4 = LOW position).
Memory: `[0x00000002][0x00000000]` = value 2^33.

Expected: 0x0000000100000000 << 1 = 0x0000000200000000 = 2^33. Correct!

Let me try another case: `a = 0x0000000100000001LL`, `b = 1`:
- `u.s.low` (HIGH) = 0x00000001, `u.s.high` (LOW) = 0x00000001
- `u.s.high` = (1 << 1) | (1 >> 31) = 2 | 0 = 2
- `u.s.low` = 1 << 1 = 2
Result: HIGH=2, LOW=2 → value 0x0000000200000002. Expected: 0x0000000200000002. Correct!

Let me try b >= 32: `a = 0x0000000100000002LL`, `b = 32`:
- `u.s.low` (HIGH) = 0x00000001, `u.s.high` (LOW) = 0x00000002
- Code: `u.s.high = (unsigned)u.s.low << (32-32) = 0x00000001 << 0 = 1`
- Code: `u.s.low = 0`

Result: HIGH=0, LOW=1 → value 0x0000000000000001. Expected: 0x0000000100000002 << 32 = 0x0000000200000000. WRONG!

Wait, let me look at the code again:

```c
if (b >= 32) {
    u.s.high = (unsigned)u.s.low << (b - 32);
    u.s.low = 0;
```

For b=32: `u.s.high = u.s.low << 0 = 0x00000001`

With PPC naming: `u.s.low`=HIGH=0x1, `u.s.high`=LOW=0x2.
After: `u.s.high`(LOW) = u.s.low(HIGH) << 0 = 0x1; `u.s.low`(HIGH) = 0.

Result: HIGH=0, LOW=1 → 0x0000000000000001.
Expected: 0x0000000100000002 << 32 = 0x0000000200000000.

This is wrong.

The correct implementation for `a << b` where b >= 32:
- result_high = a_low << (b-32)
- result_low = 0

With PPC (u.s.low=HIGH, u.s.high=LOW):
- Need to set HIGH(u.s.low) = a_low(u.s.high) << (b-32)
- Need to set LOW(u.s.high) = 0

The code does:
- Sets LOW(u.s.high) = HIGH(u.s.low) << (b-32)  ← WRONG (uses HIGH instead of LOW)
- Sets HIGH(u.s.low) = 0                          ← sets HIGH=0, should set LOW=0

So yes, the code IS wrong for PPC big-endian.

The simplest fix: go with Path B — write standalone implementations in `lib-ppc.c` using explicit 32-bit operations without the DWunion wrapper. This avoids all the endianness confusion.

6. `__fixunsdfdi(double a)` - double to unsigned long long

For float conversions, the libtcc1.c implementations (`__fixunsdfdi` and `__fixdfdi`) use `union double_long` with `EXPD(fp)` = `fp.l.upper`. With the current struct layout `struct { unsigned int lower; int upper; }`, on PPC big-endian the `lower` (offset 0) actually holds the high word of the double. So `fp.l.upper` (offset 4) holds the LOW word. But the exponent is in the HIGH word!

This is a problem for `__fixunsdfdi` too. However, the `__fixdfdi` function uses `MANTD_LL(fp)` = `(fp.ll & (HIDDEND_LL-1)) | HIDDEND_LL` which operates on the full `ll` (no endianness issue there), and `EXPD(fp)` = `(fp.l.upper >> 20) & 0x7FF` which uses `fp.l.upper` (the second struct member = LOW word on PPC big-endian). ## 🤖 Claude (Subagent)

Now let me write the implementations. First, let me check if there might be a simpler approach for `__fixunsdfdi` and `__fixdfdi` — using bitwise access to the double correctly:

On PPC big-endian, `double` in memory: `[sign|exp|mantissa_high][mantissa_low]`
- bytes 0-3: sign bit (1), exponent (11 bits), top 20 bits of mantissa
- bytes 4-7: bottom 32 bits of mantissa

With `union { double d; unsigned long long ll; }`:
- `ll` on big-endian PPC: `(high32 << 32) | low32` where high32 = `[sign|exp|mant_high]`
- Exponent = bits 62-52 of ll = `(ll >> 52) & 0x7FF`

  "old_string": "/*\n *  TCC runtime library for 32-bit PowerPC (Apple Mach-O).\n *\n *  Provides the libgcc helpers that the PPC backend emits calls to\n *  but that aren't supplied by the system. Currently just one:\n *  __floatundidf (unsigned long long -> double) which gen_cvt_itof1\n *  in tccgen.c reaches for, but Apple's libSystem doesn't export.\n *\n *  Copying and distribution of this file, with or without modification,\n *  are permitted in any medium without royalty provided the copyright\n *  notice and this notice are preserved. This file is offered as-is,\n *  without any warranty.\n */\n\n/* Convert by splitting into two 32-bit unsigned halves so we don't\n * recursively reach for __floatundidf. (uint32 -> double goes through\n * gen_cvt_itof's magic-constant inline expansion, no libcall.) */\ndouble __floatundidf(unsigned long long x)\n{\n    double hi = (double)(unsigned int)(x >> 32);\n    double lo = (double)(unsigned int)x;\n    return hi * 4294967296.0 + lo;\n}\n\n/* (Earlier sessions defined a __tcc_start_main shim here. It's now\n * obsolete: the tcc -o exe path auto-injects an equivalent helper in\n * tccelf.c::tcc_output_file when crt1.o isn't available, and the\n * gcc-link bootstrap path pulls in the real /usr/lib/crt1.o which\n * does the job properly.) */",
  "new_string": "/*\n *  TCC runtime library for 32-bit PowerPC (Apple Mach-O).\n *\n *  Provides the libgcc helpers that the PPC backend emits calls to\n *  but that aren't supplied by the system.\n *\n *  All helpers use explicit 32-bit arithmetic rather than the DWunion\n *  struct trick from libtcc1.c, because PPC is big-endian: a\n *  \"struct { int low, high; }\" has `low` at the lower address, which\n *  is the HIGH word of a big-endian long long \u2014 the naming in libtcc1.c\n *  is backwards for PPC. Simpler to just use (x >> 32) / (unsigned int)x\n *  throughout.\n *\n *  Copying and distribution of this file, with or without modification,\n *  are permitted in any medium without royalty provided the copyright\n *  notice and this notice are preserved. This file is offered as-is,\n *  without any warranty.\n */\n\n/* ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n * float helpers\n * ---------------------------------------------------------------------------*/\n\n/* Convert by splitting into two 32-bit unsigned halves so we don't\n * recursively reach for __floatundidf. (uint32 -> double goes through\n * gen_cvt_itof's magic-constant inline expansion, no libcall.) */\ndouble __floatundidf(unsigned long long x)\n{\n    double hi = (double)(unsigned int)(x >> 32);\n    double lo = (double)(unsigned int)x;\n    return hi * 4294967296.0 + lo;\n}\n\n/* double -> unsigned long long.\n *\n * IEEE 754 double layout (big-endian PPC, 8 bytes):\n *   bit 63      : sign\n *   bits 62-52  : biased exponent (bias = 1023)\n *   bits 51-0   : fraction (implicit leading 1)\n *\n * We access the raw bits via a union so we can do the extraction\n * without calling any 64-bit helpers (the union access is all\n * 32-bit loads on PPC). */\nunsigned long long __fixunsdfdi(double a)\n{\n    union { double d; unsigned int w[2]; } u;\n    unsigned int hi, lo, frac_hi, frac_lo;\n    int exp;\n\n    u.d = a;\n    /* PPC big-endian: w[0] = high 32 bits, w[1] = low 32 bits */\n    hi = u.w[0];\n    lo = u.w[1];\n\n    if (hi & 0x80000000u)   /* negative */\n        return 0;\n\n    exp = (int)((hi >> 20) & 0x7FF) - 1023;\n    if (exp < 0)\n        return 0;\n    if (exp >= 63)\n        return ~(0ULL);     /* overflow / saturate */\n\n    /* Reconstruct the 53-bit significand: implicit 1 + 52-bit fraction.\n     * frac_hi holds bits 51-32 of the fraction, frac_lo holds bits 31-0. */\n    frac_hi = (hi & 0x000FFFFF) | 0x00100000;   /* top 21 bits of significand */\n    frac_lo = lo;                                 /* bottom 32 bits */\n\n    /* The significand is a 53-bit integer Q in the range [2^52, 2^53).\n     * The value is Q * 2^(exp-52). */\n    if (exp >= 52) {\n        /* Shift left by (exp - 52). */\n        int sh = exp - 52;\n        unsigned long long q =\n            ((unsigned long long)frac_hi << 32) | (unsigned long long)frac_lo;\n        if (sh >= 32)\n            return (unsigned long long)frac_hi << (sh - 21 + 21 - 32);\n        /* careful: shift the full 53-bit value */\n        return q << sh;\n    } else {\n        /* Shift right by (52 - exp). */\n        int sh = 52 - exp;\n        unsigned long long q =\n            ((unsigned long long)frac_hi << 32) | (unsigned long long)frac_lo;\n        return q >> sh;\n    }\n}\n\n/* double -> signed long long */\nlong long __fixdfdi(double a)\n{\n    if (a < 0) {\n        unsigned long long r = __fixunsdfdi(-a);\n        return -(long long)r;\n    }\n    return (long long)__fixunsdfdi(a);\n}\n\n/* ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n * 64-bit shift helpers\n *\n * All use explicit high/low splitting to avoid the DWunion endianness trap.\n * \"high\" means the most-significant 32 bits (bits 63-32).\n * \"low\"  means the least-significant 32 bits (bits 31-0).\n * ---------------------------------------------------------------------------*/\n\nlong long __ashldi3(long long a, int b)\n{\n    unsigned int hi = (unsigned int)((unsigned long long)a >> 32);\n    unsigned int lo = (unsigned int)a;\n    unsigned int rhi, rlo;\n\n    if (b == 0) {\n        rhi = hi; rlo = lo;\n    } else if (b < 32) {\n        rhi = (hi << b) | (lo >> (32 - b));\n        rlo = lo << b;\n    } else {\n        rhi = lo << (b - 32);\n        rlo = 0;\n    }\n    return ((unsigned long long)rhi << 32) | (unsigned long long)rlo;\n}\n\nunsigned long long __lshrdi3(unsigned long long a, int b)\n{\n    unsigned int hi = (unsigned int)(a >> 32);\n    unsigned int lo = (unsigned int)a;\n    unsigned int rhi, rlo;\n\n    if (b == 0) {\n        rhi = hi; rlo = lo;\n    } else if (b < 32) {\n        rlo = (lo >> b) | (hi << (32 - b));\n        rhi = hi >> b;\n    } else {\n        rlo = hi >> (b - 32);\n        rhi = 0;\n    }\n    return ((unsigned long long)rhi << 32) | (unsigned long long)rlo;\n}\n\n/* ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n * 64-bit unsigned divide/modulo\n *\n * Simple 64-step shift-and-subtract long division.  Not fast, but\n * correct and dependency-free (no 64-bit multiply needed).\n * ---------------------------------------------------------------------------*/\n\nstatic unsigned long long __udivmoddi4_ppc(unsigned long long n,\n                                            unsigned long long d,\n                                            unsigned long long *rem)\n{\n    unsigned long long q = 0;\n    unsigned long long r = 0;\n    int i;\n\n    if (d == 0) {\n        /* Division by zero: match libgcc behaviour (return 0 / set rem=n). */\n        if (rem) *rem = n;\n        return 0;\n    }\n\n    for (i = 63; i >= 0; i--) {\n        r = (r << 1) | ((n >> i) & 1);\n        if (r >= d) {\n            r -= d;\n            q |= (unsigned long long)1 << i;\n        }\n    }\n    if (rem) *rem = r;\n    return q;\n}\n\nunsigned long long __udivdi3(unsigned long long u, unsigned long long v)\n{\n    return __udivmoddi4_ppc(u, v, (unsigned long long *)0);\n}\n\nunsigned long long __umoddi3(unsigned long long u, unsigned long long v)\n{\n    unsigned long long r;\n    __udivmoddi4_ppc(u, v, &r);\n    return r;\n}\n\n/* (Earlier sessions defined a __tcc_start_main shim here. It's now\n * obsolete: the tcc -o exe path auto-injects an equivalent helper in\n * tccelf.c::tcc_output_file when crt1.o isn't available, and the\n * gcc-link bootstrap path pulls in the real /usr/lib/crt1.o which\n * does the job properly.) */"
}

## 🤖 Claude (Subagent)


🔧 Using tool: Edit
Input: {
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "/Users/cell/claude/tcc-darwin8-ppc/tcc/lib/lib-ppc.c",
  "old_string": "/* double -> unsigned long long.\n *\n * IEEE 754 double layout (big-endian PPC, 8 bytes):\n *   bit 63      : sign\n *   bits 62-52  : biased exponent (bias = 1023)\n *   bits 51-0   : fraction (implicit leading 1)\n *\n * We access the raw bits via a union so we can do the extraction\n * without calling any 64-bit helpers (the union access is all\n * 32-bit loads on PPC). */\nunsigned long long __fixunsdfdi(double a)\n{\n    union { double d; unsigned int w[2]; } u;\n    unsigned int hi, lo, frac_hi, frac_lo;\n    int exp;\n\n    u.d = a;\n    /* PPC big-endian: w[0] = high 32 bits, w[1] = low 32 bits */\n    hi = u.w[0];\n    lo = u.w[1];\n\n    if (hi & 0x80000000u)   /* negative */\n        return 0;\n\n    exp = (int)((hi >> 20) & 0x7FF) - 1023;\n    if (exp < 0)\n        return 0;\n    if (exp >= 63)\n        return ~(0ULL);     /* overflow / saturate */\n\n    /* Reconstruct the 53-bit significand: implicit 1 + 52-bit fraction.\n     * frac_hi holds bits 51-32 of the fraction, frac_lo holds bits 31-0. */\n    frac_hi = (hi & 0x000FFFFF) | 0x00100000;   /* top 21 bits of significand */\n    frac_lo = lo;                                 /* bottom 32 bits */\n\n    /* The significand is a 53-bit integer Q in the range [2^52, 2^53).\n     * The value is Q * 2^(exp-52). */\n    if (exp >= 52) {\n        /* Shift left by (exp - 52). */\n        int sh = exp - 52;\n        unsigned long long q =\n            ((unsigned long long)frac_hi << 32) | (unsigned long long)frac_lo;\n        if (sh >= 32)\n            return (unsigned long long)frac_hi << (sh - 21 + 21 - 32);\n        /* careful: shift the full 53-bit value */\n        return q << sh;\n    } else {\n        /* Shift right by (52 - exp). */\n        int sh = 52 - exp;\n        unsigned long long q =\n            ((unsigned long long)frac_hi << 32) | (unsigned long long)frac_lo;\n        return q >> sh;\n    }\n}",
  "new_string": "/* double -> unsigned long long.\n *\n * IEEE 754 double layout (big-endian PPC, 8 bytes):\n *   bit 63      : sign\n *   bits 62-52  : biased exponent (bias = 1023)\n *   bits 51-0   : fraction (implicit leading 1)\n *\n * We access the bits via a union of two unsigned ints. PPC is big-endian,\n * so w[0] holds the high 32 bits (sign + exponent + top 20 fraction bits)\n * and w[1] holds the low 32 bits (bottom 32 fraction bits).\n *\n * All shifts here are on 32-bit values to avoid calling __ashldi3 /\n * __lshrdi3 recursively (the final assembly uses simple 32-bit shifts). */\nunsigned long long __fixunsdfdi(double a)\n{\n    union { double d; unsigned int w[2]; } u;\n    unsigned int hi, lo, sig_hi;\n    int exp, sh;\n\n    u.d = a;\n    /* PPC big-endian: w[0] = high 32 bits, w[1] = low 32 bits */\n    hi = u.w[0];\n    lo = u.w[1];\n\n    if (hi & 0x80000000u)   /* negative -> return 0 */\n        return 0;\n\n    exp = (int)((hi >> 20) & 0x7FF) - 1023;\n    if (exp < 0)\n        return 0;\n    if (exp >= 63)          /* overflow: saturate */\n        return ~(unsigned long long)0;\n\n    /* Rebuild the 53-bit significand.\n     * sig_hi = bits [52..32] of significand (implicit 1 + top 20 fraction bits)\n     * lo     = bits [31..0]  of significand */\n    sig_hi = (hi & 0x000FFFFFu) | 0x00100000u;  /* 21 significant bits */\n\n    /* Value = significand * 2^(exp-52).\n     * The significand spans bits [52..0], so:\n     *   result_hi (bits 63..32) = sig_hi shifted appropriately\n     *   result_lo (bits 31..0)  = lo shifted appropriately */\n\n    if (exp <= 20) {\n        /* Result fits in the top 21 bits of sig_hi shifted right. */\n        sh = 20 - exp;  /* shift right by this much */\n        return (unsigned long long)(sig_hi >> sh);\n    } else if (exp <= 52) {\n        /* Result spans sig_hi and lo.\n         * Shift the 53-bit number right by (52 - exp). */\n        sh = 52 - exp;\n        if (sh < 32) {\n            unsigned int rhi = sig_hi >> (sh > 20 ? sh - 21 + 21 : sh);\n            /* simpler: build 53-bit Q and shift */\n            /* sig_hi is 21 bits wide; Q = (sig_hi << 32) | lo, shift right sh */\n            /* result_hi = sig_hi >> sh (if sh <= 20), else 0 */\n            /* result_lo = (lo >> sh) | (sig_hi << (32 - sh)) */\n            unsigned int res_hi = (sh <= 20) ? (sig_hi >> sh) : 0;\n            unsigned int res_lo = (lo >> sh) | (sig_hi << (32 - sh));\n            return ((unsigned long long)res_hi << 32) | (unsigned long long)res_lo;\n        } else {\n            /* sh == 32 can't happen (exp==20 handled above); sh in [21..31] */\n            unsigned int res_lo = (lo >> sh) | (sig_hi << (32 - sh));\n            return (unsigned long long)res_lo;\n        }\n    } else {\n        /* exp > 52: shift the 53-bit number left by (exp - 52). */\n        sh = exp - 52;\n        /* Q = (sig_hi << 32) | lo; result = Q << sh */\n        /* result_hi = (sig_hi << sh) | (lo >> (32-sh))  for sh < 32  */\n        /* result_hi = lo << (sh-32)                     for sh >= 32  */\n        /* result_lo = lo << sh                          for sh < 32   */\n        /* result_lo = 0                                 for sh >= 32  */\n        if (sh < 32) {\n            unsigned int res_hi = (sig_hi << sh) | (lo >> (32 - sh));\n            unsigned int res_lo = lo << sh;\n            return ((unsigned long long)res_hi << 32) | (unsigned long long)res_lo;\n        } else {\n            /* sh in [32..62]; result_lo = 0 */\n            unsigned int res_hi = (sh < 53) ? (lo << (sh - 32)) : 0;\n            return (unsigned long long)res_hi << 32;\n        }\n    }\n}"
}

## 🤖 Claude (Subagent)

Hmm, this is getting complicated and has potential edge cases. Let me think about this differently.

The key insight is: `exp <= 20` path duplicates logic unnecessarily. Let me simplify `__fixunsdfdi` into a clean, correct implementation:

For a positive double value:
- significand = (implicit_1 << 52) | fraction = 53-bit integer
- value = significand * 2^(exp - 52) where exp = biased_exp - 1023

I need to compute this as a 64-bit integer. The significand is 53 bits. I can represent it as two 32-bit halves:
- sig_hi = bits 52..32 of significand = 21 bits (including implicit 1) 
- sig_lo = bits 31..0 of significand = 32 bits

value = ((sig_hi << 32) | sig_lo) * 2^(exp - 52) = ((sig_hi << 32) | sig_lo) << (exp-52) if exp >= 52, or >> (52-exp) if exp < 52.

The shift operations here will be on 64-bit values — but I can't call `__lshrdi3` (that would be recursive). However, I can do these shifts using 32-bit operations directly. Let me rewrite this much more cleanly:

---

PPC is big-endian,\n * so w[0] holds the high 32 bits (sign + exponent + top 20 fraction bits)\n * and w[1] holds the low 32 bits (bottom 32 fraction bits).\n *\n * All shifts here are on 32-bit values to avoid calling __ashldi3 /\n * __lshrdi3 recursively (the final assembly uses simple 32-bit shifts). */\nunsigned long long __fixunsdfdi(double a)\n{\n    union { double d; unsigned int w[2]; } u;\n    unsigned int hi, lo, sig_hi;\n    int exp, sh;\n\n    u.d = a;\n    /* PPC big-endian: w[0] = high 32 bits, w[1] = low 32 bits */\n    hi = u.w[0];\n    lo = u.w[1];\n\n    if (hi & 0x80000000u)   /* negative -> return 0 */\n        return 0;\n\n    exp = (int)((hi >> 20) & 0x7FF) - 1023;\n    if (exp < 0)\n        return 0;\n    if (exp >= 63)          /* overflow: saturate */\n        return ~(unsigned long long)0;\n\n    /* Rebuild the 53-bit significand.\n     * sig_hi = bits [52..32] of significand (implicit 1 + top 20 fraction bits)\n     * lo     = bits [31..0]  of significand */\n    sig_hi = (hi & 0x000FFFFFu) | 0x00100000u;  /* 21 significant bits */\n\n    /* Value = significand * 2^(exp-52).\n     * The significand spans bits [52..0], so:\n     *   result_hi (bits 63..32) = sig_hi shifted appropriately\n     *   result_lo (bits 31..0)  = lo shifted appropriately */\n\n    if (exp <= 20) {\n        /* Result fits in the top 21 bits of sig_hi shifted right. */\n        sh = 20 - exp;  /* shift right by this much */\n        return (unsigned long long)(sig_hi >> sh);\n    } else if (exp <= 52) {\n        /* Result spans sig_hi and lo.\n         * Shift the 53-bit number right by (52 - exp). */\n        sh = 52 - exp;\n        if (sh < 32) {\n            unsigned int rhi = sig_hi >> (sh > 20 ? sh - 21 + 21 : sh);\n            /* simpler: build 53-bit Q and shift */\n            /* sig_hi is 21 bits wide; Q = (sig_hi << 32) | lo, shift right sh */\n            /* result_hi = sig_hi >> sh (if sh <= 20), else 0 */\n            /* result_lo = (lo >> sh) | (sig_hi << (32 - sh)) */\n            unsigned int res_hi = (sh <= 20) ? (sig_hi >> sh) : 0;\n            unsigned int res_lo = (lo >> sh) | (sig_hi << (32 - sh));\n            return ((unsigned long long)res_hi << 32) | (unsigned long long)res_lo;\n        } else {\n            /* sh == 32 can't happen (exp==20 handled above); sh in [21..31] */\n            unsigned int res_lo = (lo >> sh) | (sig_hi << (32 - sh));\n            return (unsigned long long)res_lo;\n        }\n    } else {\n        /* exp > 52: shift the 53-bit number left by (exp - 52). */\n        sh = exp - 52;\n        /* Q = (sig_hi << 32) | lo; result = Q << sh */\n        /* result_hi = (sig_hi << sh) | (lo >> (32-sh))  for sh < 32  */\n        /* result_hi = lo << (sh-32)                     for sh >= 32  */\n        /* result_lo = lo << sh                          for sh < 32   */\n        /* result_lo = 0                                 for sh >= 32  */\n        if (sh < 32) {\n            unsigned int res_hi = (sig_hi << sh) | (lo >> (32 - sh));\n            unsigned int res_lo = lo << sh;\n            return ((unsigned long long)res_hi << 32) | (unsigned long long)res_lo;\n        } else {\n            /* sh in [32..62]; result_lo = 0 */\n            unsigned int res_hi = (sh < 53) ? (lo << (sh - 32)) : 0;\n            return (unsigned long long)res_hi << 32;\n        }\n    }\n}",
  "new_string": "/* double -> unsigned long long.\n *\n * IEEE 754 double layout (big-endian PPC, 8 bytes):\n *   bit 63      : sign\n *   bits 62-52  : biased exponent (bias = 1023)\n *   bits 51-0   : fraction (implicit leading 1)\n *\n * We access the bits via a union of two unsigned ints. PPC is big-endian,\n * so w[0] holds the high 32 bits (sign + exponent + top 20 fraction bits)\n * and w[1] holds the low 32 bits (bottom 32 fraction bits).\n *\n * All bit-assembly is done with 32-bit values to avoid calling __ashldi3 /\n * __lshrdi3 recursively. */\nunsigned long long __fixunsdfdi(double a)\n{\n    union { double d; unsigned int w[2]; } u;\n    unsigned int hi, lo, sig_hi, sig_lo;\n    unsigned int res_hi, res_lo;\n    int exp, sh;\n\n    u.d = a;\n    /* PPC big-endian: w[0] = high 32 bits, w[1] = low 32 bits */\n    hi = u.w[0];\n    lo = u.w[1];\n\n    if (hi & 0x80000000u)   /* negative -> 0 */\n        return 0;\n\n    exp = (int)((hi >> 20) & 0x7FF) - 1023;\n    if (exp < 0)\n        return 0;\n    if (exp >= 64)          /* overflow: saturate to ULLONG_MAX */\n        return ~(unsigned long long)0;\n\n    /* The 53-bit significand Q = (1 << 52) | fraction.\n     * Represent it as two halves:\n     *   sig_hi = bits [52..32] = 21 bits (bit 20 is the implicit 1)\n     *   sig_lo = bits [31..0]  = 32 bits\n     * so Q = (sig_hi << 32) | sig_lo (conceptually a 64-bit value). */\n    sig_hi = (hi & 0x000FFFFFu) | 0x00100000u;\n    sig_lo = lo;\n\n    /* result = Q * 2^(exp - 52)\n     * = Q >> (52 - exp)  if exp <= 52\n     * = Q << (exp - 52)  if exp >  52 */\n\n    if (exp <= 52) {\n        sh = 52 - exp;\n        /* right-shift a 53-bit number (fits in 21+32 bits) by sh [0..52] */\n        if (sh >= 53) {\n            res_hi = 0; res_lo = 0;\n        } else if (sh >= 32) {\n            /* sh in [32..52]: sig_lo is shifted out entirely */\n            res_hi = 0;\n            res_lo = sig_hi >> (sh - 32);\n        } else {\n            /* sh in [0..31] */\n            res_hi = sig_hi >> sh;      /* high part: only valid when sh <= 20 */\n            res_lo = (sig_lo >> sh) | (sig_hi << (32 - sh));\n            /* If sh > 20 then sig_hi >> sh == 0, which is correct (result < 2^32). */\n        }\n    } else {\n        sh = exp - 52;\n        /* left-shift a 53-bit value by sh [1..11] (exp is at most 63) */\n        if (sh >= 32) {\n            /* sh in [32..11]: sig_hi goes to res_hi shifted further,\n             * sig_lo contributes to res_hi only */\n            res_hi = sig_lo << (sh - 32);\n            res_lo = 0;\n        } else {\n            /* sh in [1..31] */\n            res_hi = (sig_hi << sh) | (sig_lo >> (32 - sh));\n            res_lo = sig_lo << sh;\n        }\n    }\n    return ((unsigned long long)res_hi << 32) | (unsigned long long)res_lo;\n}"

Wait: for `a >> 32`, the result is: bits 63-32 shifted to 31-0, and top 32 bits become 0. So result = hi (as a 64-bit value). That's `(0 << 32) | hi`. My code gives `rlo = hi >> 0 = hi`, `rhi = 0`. So result = `(0 << 32) | hi` = hi as uint64. Correct!

What about b=31?

Now let me also check `__fixdfdi` — it calls `__fixunsdfdi(-a)` for negative. Does `-a` work for doubles? Yes, negating a double is a single instruction on PPC, no helpers needed.

Let me also verify the `__udivmoddi4_ppc` implementation. The shift-subtract loop with 64 iterations should be correct but slow. Let me trace a simple case: `9 / 3 = 3`:
- n=9, d=3
- Initialize q=0, r=0
- i=63..3: r stays 0 since n's bits are 0 in those positions; all produce r<d so q bits remain 0
- i=3: r = (0<<1) | ((9>>3)&1) = 0|1 = 1; 1 < 3, no subtract
- i=2: r = (1<<1) | ((9>>2)&1) = 2|0 = 2; 2 < 3, no subtract  
- Wait, 9 = 0b1001, so bit 3 = 1, bit 2 = 0, bit 1 = 0, bit 0 = 1.
- i=3: r = 0*2 | ((9>>3)&1) = 1; 1 < 3
- i=2: r = 1*2 | ((9>>2)&1) = 2 | 0 = 2; 2 < 3
- i=1: r = 2*2 | ((9>>1)&1) = 4 | 0 = 4; 4 >= 3, so r = 4-3 = 1, q |= 1<<1 = 2
- i=0: r = 1*2 | ((9>>0)&1) = 2 | 1 = 3; 3 >= 3, so r = 0, q |= 1<<0 = 3
- Result: q=3, r=0. Correct!

The loop is correct. It's O(64) always, but for a runtime helper it's acceptable.

Now let me also check a potential issue: the 64-bit shift operations within `__udivmoddi4_ppc`. The line `r = (r << 1) | ((n >> i) & 1)` uses 64-bit shifts. When compiled by tcc on PPC, `<<` and `>>` on `unsigned long long` will call `__ashldi3` and `__lshrdi3`... which we're also defining in this same file. Will there be a circular dependency?

TCC compiles each function independently. Since `__ashldi3` and `__lshrdi3` are defined in lib-ppc.c and don't use any 64-bit shifts internally (they use only 32-bit operations), they compile fine without needing `__ashldi3`/`__lshrdi3`. Then `__udivmoddi4_ppc` uses `r << 1` and `n >> i` — those calls to `__ashldi3`/`__lshrdi3` refer to symbols that will be resolved at link time from the same libtcc1.a. Since libtcc1.a is a static archive, all symbols are available when we link tcc-self.

But there's a subtlety: when building libtcc1.a itself, `lib-ppc.o` is being compiled by tcc. At that point, the 64-bit shift in `__udivmoddi4_ppc` will generate calls to `__ashldi3`/`__lshrdi3`. Those symbols are defined in the SAME object file (lib-ppc.c). So they'll be available in the same .o file and linked together. Good — no circular issue since within the same compilation unit, the function bodies are emitted into the same .o.

## 🤖 Claude (Subagent)

This confirms: when the shift count is a **constant**, tcc inlines the 64-bit shift using 32-bit operations. When the shift count is a **variable** (runtime), tcc calls `__ashldi3`/`__lshrdi3`/`__ashrdi3`.

In `__udivmoddi4_ppc`, I have:
- `r = (r << 1)` — shift by constant 1 → **inlined** by tcc! No call to `__ashldi3`.
- `(n >> i)` — shift by variable `i` → calls `__lshrdi3`!

So `__udivmoddi4_ppc` calling `__lshrdi3` at runtime IS a dependency. But that's fine because `__lshrdi3` is defined in the same object file (`lib-ppc.c`), so it'll be in the same `.o` and available at link time.

However, there's a recursive concern: when tcc compiles `__lshrdi3` in lib-ppc.c, it uses only 32-bit shifts (all `>> b` and `<< (32-b)` where the values are 32-bit `unsigned int`). So tcc won't emit calls to `__lshrdi3` when compiling `__lshrdi3` itself. Good.

But wait — in `__udivmoddi4_ppc`, the line `r = (r << 1) | ((n >> i) & 1)` — when compiled by tcc:
- `r << 1`: constant shift by 1 on 64-bit `r` → inlined
- `n >> i`: variable shift → calls `__lshrdi3`
- `(n >> i) & 1`: result is 64-bit, then `& 1` gives 64-bit result
- `r = ... | ...`: 64-bit OR → inlined

And `if (r >= d)`: 64-bit comparison → may call a helper? Let me check:

## 🤖 Claude (Subagent)

Wait, I need to reconsider the 64-bit shifts within `__udivmoddi4_ppc`. Looking at the loop:

```c
r = (r << 1) | ((n >> i) & 1);
```

- `r << 1`: constant shift by 1 on 64-bit `r` — tcc inlines this
- `n >> i`: **variable** shift — tcc calls `__lshrdi3`
- `(unsigned long long)1 << i`: **variable** shift — tcc calls `__ashldi3`

So this function uses `__lshrdi3` and `__ashldi3`. Those are defined above in the same file. Since all functions end up in the same `.o` file (`lib-ppc.o`), there's no issue.

But there's another subtlety: `r << 1` is a constant-count 64-bit left shift. In tccgen.c, constant-count llong shifts are inlined. But inlining works by splitting into two 32-bit words. This uses tcc's internal logic. Let me verify this works for PPC:

Looking at tccgen.c lines 2266-2316, the constant-count case handles llong shifts by: lexpand → manipulate high/low → lbuild. This is an internal tcc operation that uses tcc's own register handling. Whether it works correctly on PPC depends on tcc's internal `lexpand`/`lbuild` being correct.

Since the project (session 025) says tcc can already compile tcc.c (which uses 64-bit operations), the inline 64-bit shift mechanism works. So `r << 1` being inlined is fine.

The other issue: `(unsigned long long)1 << i` — the literal `1` is a constant, but `i` is a variable. This is a variable-count shift, so tcc calls `__ashldi3`. That's fine.

Also: `r -= d` is 64-bit subtraction. Does tcc have a subtract helper? Looking at tccgen.c — subtraction uses SUBC1/SUBC2 opcodes (carry-based subtract) which are inlined. Good.

`r >= d` is 64-bit unsigned comparison — inlined as we saw above.

`q |= ...` is 64-bit OR — inlined.

Everything looks OK. But let me double-check one thing: in `__ashldi3`, I have:

```c
unsigned int hi = (unsigned int)((unsigned long long)a >> 32);
```

This is a constant shift by 32 on a 64-bit value. Tcc will inline this as: `high_word = a_high_register`. Let me verify this is the correct extraction of the high word.

When tcc stores a `long long a` parameter on PPC (big-endian), the ABI places r3 = HIGH word, r4 = LOW word (from the session 025 notes: "Apple PPC ABI returns r3=HIGH, r4=LOW"). So internally tcc's lexpand gives H = high word (r3) and L = low word (r4).

A constant `>> 32` on big-endian llong in tccgen.c:
- Stack: L H shift(=32)
- c=32 >= 32: pop shift, swap if SHR: stack = H L (after swap for SHR)
- Wait, let me re-read more carefully:

Actually for `a >> 32` where a is `unsigned long long`, `(unsigned long long)a >> 32`:
- After lexpand: stack has L then H
- For SHR (logical right shift): swap → H is top, L is below
- Actually I need to trace the tccgen.c code more carefully. But this is getting very deep. Let me just trust that tcc's existing constant-count llong shifts work correctly (they must, given tcc.c compiles successfully), and focus on whether my 32-bit extraction is semantically correct.

When tcc sees `(unsigned long long)a >> 32`, it inlines this as: result = { 0, high_word_of_a }. On PPC, the "high word" is r3. `(unsigned int)((unsigned long long)a >> 32)` truncates to 32 bits, giving r3. That's the high 32 bits of `a`, which I store in `hi`. This is semantically correct.

`(unsigned int)a` — truncation of long long to int: takes the low 32 bits = r4. Stored in `lo`. Correct.

Similarly for the reconstruction:
`((unsigned long long)rhi << 32) | (unsigned long long)rlo`
= `(rhi, rlo)` as a 64-bit big-endian value. Correct.

Great, the implementation looks sound. Now let me also check: is there a `__ashrdi3` needed? Looking at the list of missing symbols: `___ashldi3`, `___fixdfdi`, `___fixunsdfdi`, `___lshrdi3`, `___udivdi3`, `___umoddi3`. No `__ashrdi3`. But let me add it anyway since it's a standard helper that might be needed in general. Actually, the task says to be surgical and not add unnecessary things. I'll leave it out.

But wait — could tcc.c use arithmetic right shift on signed long long somewhere that generates `__ashrdi3`? Let me check if it's in the missing symbol list: No, `___ashrdi3` is NOT in the list from the task description. So let's not add it.

Wait actually — do I need `libtcc1.o`? My plan is to add everything to `lib-ppc.c` (Path B approach), so `lib-ppc.o` already handles all the helpers. I DON'T need to add `libtcc1.o`.

The Makefile already has:
```

```

Since `lib-ppc.o` is already there, and I added all helpers to `lib-ppc.c`, no Makefile change is needed.

Wait, let me re-read the task: "Files to modify: `tcc/lib/Makefile` — add `libtcc1.o` to `OBJ-ppc-osx`". But since I'm taking Path B (adding helpers directly to lib-ppc.c), I don't need to modify the Makefile at all. The Makefile already compiles lib-ppc.c into libtcc1.a.

The relevant line is:
```

```

And `OBJ-libtcc1 = $(addprefix $(X),$(filter-out $(OBJ-extra),$(OBJ-$T)))` which for `T=ppc-osx` gives `lib-ppc.o` (since OSX_O is empty). So `libtcc1.a` for ppc-osx includes `lib-ppc.o`. Good, no Makefile change needed.

Exit code 138 = 128 + 10 = SIGBUS. The program is crashing with a bus error, which suggests a misaligned access or bad pointer. Let me look at what's different.

The problem might be in `__fixunsdfdi`. The union `{ double d; unsigned int w[2]; }` — on PPC, when we access `u.w[0]`, what address alignment does this have? `double` requires 8-byte alignment. `unsigned int w[2]` requires 4-byte alignment. The union is aligned to 8 bytes. `w[0]` is at offset 0 (aligned), `w[1]` is at offset 4 (aligned). This should be fine.

But wait — what does tcc do with this union? Let me think about whether tcc correctly handles union type-punning between `double` and `unsigned int[2]`.

Actually, there might be a simpler issue: tcc might not know how to access `u.w[0]` correctly when the union contains a double. The double occupies 8 bytes, and the array is 8 bytes. But does tcc handle `u.w[0]` as a load from the union's address?

## 🤖 Claude (Subagent)

Hmm, `fixdfdi(-3.7)` should return -3 (truncation toward zero), but it returns 0! There's a bug. Let me check:

`__fixdfdi(-3.7)` calls `__fixunsdfdi(3.7)`:
- `3.7` in IEEE 754: sign=0, exp = 1023+1 = 0x400, fraction = 0xD999... 
- Actually, 3.7 = 1.85 * 2^1, so biased exp = 1024
- Wait: 3.7 = 1.1 * 2^1 (approximately), biased exp = 1 + 1023 = 1024
- hi word: 0 | (1024 << 20) | (fraction_hi) 

Let me be more precise: 3.7 in binary is approximately 11.1011001100... 
= 1.11011001100... × 2^1
So biased exp = 1024 = 0x400
hi = 0x400D9999... wait let me compute:

3.7 = 3 + 0.7. 3 = 11 binary. 0.7 = 0.1011001100... binary (recurring).
So 3.7 ≈ 1.1101100110011... × 2^1

biased_exp = 1024 = 0x400
hi word: (0x400 << 20) | (0xD9999... top 20 bits of 0xD9999...)
= 0x400D9999 (approximately)

Actually: 0x400D_9999_9999_9999A is the hex of 3.7 in IEEE754.
So hi = 0x400D9999, lo = 0x9999999A.

With my code:
- `hi & 0x80000000` = 0 (positive, good)
- `exp = (0x400D9999 >> 20) & 0x7FF = 0x400 & 0x7FF = 1024 - 1023 = 1`
- `exp` = 1, which is >= 0 and < 64, good
- `sig_hi = (0x400D9999 & 0x000FFFFF) | 0x00100000 = 0x000D9999 | 0x00100000 = 0x001D9999`
- `sig_lo = 0x9999999A`
- `exp <= 52`: `sh = 52 - 1 = 51`
- `sh >= 32`: `res_hi = 0`, `res_lo = sig_hi >> (51 - 32) = sig_hi >> 19 = 0x001D9999 >> 19 = 3`
- Result = 3. That should be correct!

But we got 0. Let me check if the issue is in `__fixdfdi`:

```c
long long __fixdfdi(double a) {
    if (a < 0) {
        unsigned long long r = __fixunsdfdi(-a);
        return -(long long)r;
    }
    return (long long)__fixunsdfdi(a);
}
```

For `a = -3.7`, `a < 0` is true, so we call `__fixunsdfdi(3.7)`. If that returns 3, we return `-3`. But we got 0.

Maybe the issue is that `__fixunsdfdi` returns 0 for 3.7 when compiled by tcc on PPC. Let me test directly:

## 🤖 Claude (Subagent)

`fixdfdi(-42.9)` returns 0 instead of -42. The issue is in my `__fixdfdi`. Let me trace more carefully:

The function calls `__fixunsdfdi(-a)`. When `a = -42.9`, `-a = 42.9`. Let me test:

---

## 🤖 Claude (Subagent)


🔧 Using tool: Bash
Input: {
  "command": "ssh ibookg37 'cd ~/tcc-darwin8-ppc && cat > /tmp/test_fix3.c << '\"'\"'EOF'\"'\"'\nint printf(const char *fmt, ...);\ntypedef unsigned long long ULL;\ntypedef long long LL;\nextern ULL __fixunsdfdi(double a);\nint main(void) {\n    double a = 42.9;\n    double b = -42.9;\n    printf(\"fixunsdfdi(42.9) = %llu\\n\", __fixunsdfdi(a));\n    printf(\"fixunsdfdi(-42.9) = %llu\\n\", __fixunsdfdi(b));\n    /* pass negative directly */\n    printf(\"fixunsdfdi(-42.9 literal) = %llu\\n\", __fixunsdfdi(-42.9));\n    return 0;\n}\nEOF\n./tcc/tcc -B./tcc -I./tcc -o /tmp/test_fix3 /tmp/test_fix3.c ./tcc/libtcc1.a 2>&1\n/tmp/test_fix3'",
  "description": "Diagnose fixdfdi negative value issue",
  "timeout": 30000
}


---

## 🤖 Claude (Subagent)

`__fixunsdfdi(42.9) = 42` is correct. But `__fixunsdfdi(-42.9) = 0` is correct — negative doubles should return 0 from `__fixunsdfdi` (that's the expected behavior for unsigned conversion). So the issue in `__fixdfdi` is not with `__fixunsdfdi` returning wrong values.

The problem is in how `__fixdfdi` passes `-a` to `__fixunsdfdi`. Let me look more carefully:

```c
long long __fixdfdi(double a) {
    if (a < 0) {
        unsigned long long r = __fixunsdfdi(-a);
        return -(long long)r;
    }
    return (long long)__fixunsdfdi(a);
}
```

The issue is that `-a` where `a` is a function parameter of type `double` — when `a = -42.9`, `-a` should be `42.9`. But given the broken float printf we saw, maybe tcc-on-PPC has a bug with unary negation of double values loaded from the stack!

Let me test this specific pattern:

---

## 🤖 Claude (Subagent)


🔧 Using tool: Bash
Input: {
  "command": "ssh ibookg37 'cd ~/tcc-darwin8-ppc && cat > /tmp/test_neg.c << '\"'\"'EOF'\"'\"'\nint printf(const char *fmt, ...);\ntypedef unsigned long long ULL;\nULL bits_of_double(double x) {\n    union { double d; ULL u; } v;\n    v.d = x;\n    return v.u;\n}\ndouble neg_double(double x) { return -x; }\nint main(void) {\n    double a = -42.9;\n    printf(\"bits of -42.9: %llx\\n\", bits_of_double(a));\n    double b = neg_double(a);\n    printf(\"bits of neg(-42.9): %llx\\n\", bits_of_double(b));\n    /* expected: -42.9 ~= 0xC045733333333333 */\n    /* expected neg: 42.9 ~= 0x4045733333333333 */\n    return 0;\n}\nEOF\n./tcc/tcc -B./tcc -I./tcc -o /tmp/test_neg /tmp/test_neg.c 2>&1\n/tmp/test_neg'",
  "description": "Test double negation at the bit level",
  "timeout": 30000
}


---

## 🤖 Claude (Subagent)

`bits of neg(-42.9)` = `c0457333333333b3`. But it should be `4045733333333333`. The sign bit wasn't flipped! `c0457333333333b3` vs `c045733333333333` — only a tiny difference in the low bits, not a sign flip. The double negation is buggy in tcc-on-PPC.

This is a pre-existing tcc-on-PPC bug that's separate from what I'm implementing. The `__fixdfdi` function relies on `a < 0` and `-a`, which are both broken.

Let me work around this by not using `-a` in `__fixdfdi`. Instead, I can flip the sign bit directly:

---

## 🤖 Claude (Subagent)


🔧 Using tool: Edit
Input: {
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "/Users/cell/claude/tcc-darwin8-ppc/tcc/lib/lib-ppc.c",
  "old_string": "/* double -> signed long long */\nlong long __fixdfdi(double a)\n{\n    if (a < 0) {\n        unsigned long long r = __fixunsdfdi(-a);\n        return -(long long)r;\n    }\n    return (long long)__fixunsdfdi(a);\n}",
  "new_string": "/* double -> signed long long.\n *\n * We deliberately avoid unary negation of the double argument because\n * tcc-on-PPC has a codegen bug where `fneg` on a parameter double can\n * corrupt the low bits (the sign bit is not always correctly flipped).\n * Instead, we flip the sign bit manually in the raw bit representation. */\nlong long __fixdfdi(double a)\n{\n    union { double d; unsigned int w[2]; } u;\n    unsigned int hi;\n    int negative;\n\n    u.d = a;\n    hi = u.w[0];                        /* PPC big-endian: w[0] = high 32 bits */\n    negative = (hi & 0x80000000u) != 0;\n\n    if (negative) {\n        /* Clear sign bit to get |a|, then convert unsigned, then negate. */\n        u.w[0] = hi & ~0x80000000u;\n        return -(long long)__fixunsdfdi(u.d);\n    }\n    return (long long)__fixunsdfdi(a);\n}"
}


---

## 🤖 Claude (Subagent)

Wait, but this creates a chicken-and-egg problem again: inside `__fixdfdi`, I'm writing to `u.w[0]` and then reading `u.d`. This is a double stored in a union. When tcc accesses `u.d` as a double, will it correctly load the 8-byte value from the union? Let me think...

On PPC, `u` is 8 bytes, aligned to 8 bytes. `u.w[0]` is an `unsigned int` at offset 0. Writing to `u.w[0]` via a store word. Then `u.d` reads the full 8-byte double via `lfd`. The store and load overlap — this is type-punning via union, which is valid in C99. Tcc should handle this correctly since the union is on the stack.

But there's still the concern: does tcc on PPC correctly load a double from the stack after we stored to its component integers? Let me check if there are any tcc-on-PPC issues with this pattern:

Actually, the real question is: does tcc on PPC have a bug with `__fixdfdi` being called from tcc.c? Let me first check what tcc.c actually uses. The missing symbols were identified as `___ashldi3`, `___fixdfdi`, `___fixunsdfdi`, `___lshrdi3`, `___udivdi3`, `___umoddi3`. The test sequence shows they're needed when linking `tcc.o`. Whether `__fixdfdi` is called with a negative argument at runtime is another matter.

This strongly suggests the crash is related to how crt1 was compiled into the binary. In crt1.o, the references to `_dyld_*` functions and the `__dyld` section are expressed as relocations. When tcc loads crt1.o and processes its relocations, it places the `__dyld` section at `0x77158`. ## 🤖 Claude (Subagent)

OK! So `__DATA,__dyld` is mapped to `.data` in the ELF-internal representation. The 8-byte placeholder from crt1 gets folded into `.data`. But then the EXE writer creates a NEW `__dyld` section at a different location!

This is the bug! Here's what happens:

1. `macho_load_object_file` loads crt1.o. Its `__DATA,__dyld` section (which contains the `_dyld_func_lookup` and `__dyld_stub_binding_helper` pointers — 8 bytes) is merged into tcc's `.data` section. The crt1 relocations that reference `__DATA,__dyld` create anchor symbols pointing into `.data` at the merged location.

2. The EXE writer (`macho_output_exe`) creates a fresh `__dyld` section at `data_cur` (end of `__nl_symbol_ptr`). This new `__dyld` section is 8 bytes of zeros.

3. The crt1 code's references to `__DATA,__dyld` go through the anchor symbols (relocs were resolved to the `.data` address), but crt1 also has static references that the EXE writer should have the `__dyld` section at a fixed address. But crt1's section references go through relocations that were patched to point to `.data` (where the 8 bytes were loaded from crt1.o).

4. The separate `__dyld` section created by the EXE writer is EMPTY/unrelated to what crt1 expects.

Actually wait — let me think again. The `__dyld` section in the EXE writer is 8 bytes at some address. crt1 references to it are through HA16/LO16 pairs (which are section-relative relocations). When macho_load_object_file processes these relocations, it resolves them to addresses in `.data` (since `__dyld` is mapped to `.data`). The resolved virtual addresses would then be whatever offset in `.data` the dyld bytes were placed at.

But the EXE writer also creates a separate `__dyld` section. This section contains 8 bytes of zeros that dyld will write to. The section itself at a specific VA is what dyld looks for.

Let me check what goes in the EXE writer's `__dyld` section:

---

## 🤖 Claude (Subagent)


🔧 Using tool: Read
Input: {
  "file_path": "/Users/cell/claude/tcc-darwin8-ppc/tcc/ppc-macho.c",
  "offset": 2290,
  "limit": 20
}


---

## 🤖 Claude (Subagent)

OK so the EXE writer writes 8 zeros for `__dyld`. This is correct — dyld needs that section to exist at a specific address.

But the issue is that crt1's ORIGINAL 8-byte `__dyld` content (from the .o file) has been merged into `.data` by the Mach-O reader. The 8 bytes in `.data` are the original values from crt1's `__DATA,__dyld` section. Let me check what crt1's `__dyld` section contains originally:

---

🔧 Using tool: Bash

}


---

## 🤖 Claude (Subagent)

The crt1.o `__dyld` section contains `[0x8fe01000, 0x8fe01008]` — these are addresses in dyld's own address space (0x8fe01000 is where dyld lives on Tiger PPC). These are the function pointers to `_dyld_func_lookup` and `__dyld_stub_binding_helper` that dyld writes in.

But wait — these are the INITIAL values that dyld PROVIDES. 1. The section exists at the right address in the binary
2. dyld finds it and writes the correct function pointers

## 🤖 Claude (Subagent)

`__DATA,__dyld` in crt1.o is at section address `0x00000500`, size 8 bytes. The HA16/LO16 pair relocations for `__dyld` with `other_half = 0x0000`:

For HA16: `target_value = (this_half << 16) + (int16_t)0x0000 = (this_half << 16)`

The HA16 value of `0x500`:
- `HA(0x500) = (0x500 + 0x8000) >> 16 = 0x8500 >> 16 = 0` (since 0x500 < 0x8000)
- But the `__dyld` section is at addr `0x00000500` in crt1.o

That's wrong. But wait — `addr` of the crt1.o `__dyld` section is the virtual address within crt1.o's address space. The HA16/LO16 pair in crt1 reconstructs the original address `0x500` as:
- HA: insn immediate = `(0x500 + 0x8000) >> 16 = 0` (this would be 0 since 0x500 doesn't overflow 0x8000)
- But `(0x500 >> 16) = 0`. So the HA16 instruction would have immediate = 0, and LO16 = 0x500.

Actually for HA16/LO16 pair in the Mach-O reader:
```c
if (r_type == PPC_RELOC_HA16) {
    target_value = ((uint32_t)this_half << 16) + (int16_t)other_half;
```

`this_half` = HA instruction immediate = `(0x500 + 0x8000) >> 16 = 0`
`other_half` = pair (LO16) = `0x500 & 0xFFFF = 0x500`
`target_value = (0 << 16) + (int16_t)0x500 = 0x500` ✓

Now I can see the issue clearly!

Looking at the `__dyld` relocations:

Wait, the format shows:
```
offset   HA16  r_symnum=9  PAIR half=0x0500    ← HA16 pair (LO gives 0x0500)
```

So for the HA16 at `0x380`: `other_half = 0x0500`. And crt1.o's `__dyld` section is at addr `0x500`. So:
- HA16 of 0x500: `(0x500 + 0x8000) >> 16 = 0` → insn immediate = 0
- `target_value = (0 << 16) + (int16_t)0x0500 = 0x500` ✓

For the LO16 at `0x384`: `other_half = 0x0000` (pair for LO16 gives HA part = 0).
- LO16 of 0x500: `0x500 & 0xffff = 0x500` → insn immediate = 0x500
- But wait, in the formula: `target_value = ((uint32_t)other_half << 16) + (int16_t)this_half`
- `this_half = 0x500`, `other_half = 0x0000`
- `target_value = 0 + 0x500 = 0x500` ✓

Good, the first HA16/LO16 pair correctly gives `target_value = 0x500`.

For the second pair (offsets `0x398` HA16 and `0x39c` LO16):
- HA16 at `0x398`: `other_half = 0x0504`
- LO16 at `0x39c`: `other_half = 0x0000`

For HA16: `target_value = (this_ha16_immediate << 16) + (int16_t)0x0504`. 
The target is `0x500 + 4 = 0x504` (second word of `__dyld`).
- `HA(0x504) = 0`, insn immediate = 0
- `target_value = (0 << 16) + (int16_t)0x0504 = 0x504` ✓

And LO16 at `0x39c` with `other_half = 0x0000`:
- LO16 of 0x504: insn immediate = 0x504
- `target_value = 0 + 0x504 = 0x504` ✓

OK so both HA16/LO16 pairs in crt1.o correctly reference offsets 0 and 4 within `__DATA,__dyld`. The `target_value` is `0x500` and `0x504`.

Now in the Mach-O reader, for a non-extern section relocation to `__DATA,__dyld` (section 9):
```c
Section *target_tcc = ctx->sec_to_tcc[target_sect];  // = ".data"
uint32_t merged_off;
merged_off = ctx->sec_to_off[target_sect]
           + (target_value - ctx->sects[target_sect].addr);
           // = offset where __dyld was merged into .data
           //   + (0x500 - 0x500) = sec_to_off[__dyld]
           // Or for target_value = 0x504:
           //   + (0x504 - 0x500) = sec_to_off[__dyld] + 4
```

`ctx->sects[target_sect].addr` = `0x500` (crt1's `__dyld` section address).
`merged_off = ctx->sec_to_off[9] + 0` for first pair.

The anchor symbol has `st_value = merged_off` within `.data`. In the final linked binary, `.data` vmaddr = `0x77000`. So the anchor's final VA = `0x77000 + merged_off`.

The `merged_off` is whatever offset the 8 `__dyld` bytes landed at in the merged `.data`. From the output dump of tcc-self2's `__data`:
```
00077000: 00061724 00000000 00000000 00000000
00077010: 00000000 0008c830 8fe01000 8fe01008
```

The bytes `8fe01000 8fe01008` are at `0x7701c`-`0x7701f`. But `merged_off` should be `0x1c` for the first pair.

Now the final virtual address: `0x77000 + 0x1c = 0x7701c`.

But in the EXE, the HA16/LO16 pair produces: `lis r2, 0x7; lwz r2, 0x7154(r2)` = `0x70000 + 0x7154 = 0x77154`.

`0x77154 ≠ 0x7701c`. The HA16/LO16 instructions have the wrong values!

So there's a bug in how the HA16/LO16 relocation is applied in the EXE writer. The anchor symbol has the correct `st_value = 0x7701c`, but when the EXE writer processes the `R_PPC_ADDR16_HA` relocation against this symbol, it computes:

Actually, the key question is: in the TCC-compiled `tcc.c`, what sections does it emit for data? Looking at how tcc works: when compiling `tcc.c`, tcc outputs into:
- `.text` for code
- `.data` for initialized global/static data
- `.bss` for uninitialized data
- `.data.ro` for read-only data (strings, constants)

But tcc's Mach-O output section names are:
- `__text` for `.text`
- `__const` for `.data.ro`
- `__data` for `.data`
- `__bss` for `.bss`

So initialized data from tcc.c goes to `__DATA,__data`. But the size is only 32 bytes! That seems impossibly small. Unless most of tcc.c's "data" is actually in `.bss` (zero-initialized), which would explain the tiny `.data` size.

Actually yes — in C, uninitialized global variables go to `.bss`, and initialized-to-zero variables could go to either `.data` (initialized) or `.bss`. TCC might put zero-initialized data in `.bss`. Let me check the bss section size:

```
__bss: addr 0x78000, size 0x14868 = ~82KB
```

Yes, 82KB of `.bss`. That makes sense for tcc's internal tables and state.

Now I can see clearly:
- `0x6e1c0`: `lis r12, 0x7; lwz r12, 0x7018(r12)` — this accesses `0x77018`
- `0x6e1d8`: `lis r11, 0x7; lwz r11, 0x701c(r11)` — this accesses `0x7701c`

And `0x6df24`: `lwz r2, 0x7154(r2)` — accesses `0x77154`.

So there are THREE separate accesses:
1. `0x6e1c0`: loads from `0x77018` → offset `0x18` into `.data` → the first dyld byte `0x8fe01000`
2. `0x6e1d8`: loads from `0x7701c` → offset `0x1c` into `.data` → the second dyld byte `0x8fe01008`

## 🤖 Claude (Subagent)

HA16 at `0xdc`, PAIR half = `0x04e4`. LO16 at `0xe4`, PAIR half = `0x0000`.

`target_value` for HA16: `(this_half << 16) + (int16_t)0x04e4`. The `this_half` is `((0x4e4 + 0x8000) >> 16) = 0`. So `target_value = (0 << 16) + (int16_t)0x04e4 = 0x4e4`. ✓

`target_value` for LO16: `(other_half << 16) + (int16_t)this_half = (0x04e4 << 16) + (int16_t)0x4e4 = ... wait no, the LO16 formula is:
```c
target_value = ((uint32_t)other_half << 16) + (int16_t)this_half
```
- `other_half` = PAIR half = `0x0000`
- `this_half` = LO16 instruction immediate = `0x4e4`
- `target_value = 0 + (int16_t)0x4e4 = 0x4e4` ✓

So both relocations have `target_value = 0x4e4` which is the `__DATA,__nl_symbol_ptr` section's address in crt1.o.

Now in the Mach-O reader: `target_sect = 7 - 1 = 6` (0-indexed). `ctx->sects[6].addr = 0x4e4`. `ctx->sec_to_tcc[6]` = the TCC section for `__DATA,__nl_symbol_ptr`... what section is that?

## 🤖 Claude (Subagent)

`_mach_init_routine` is at `__nl_symbol_ptr` slot `0x77154`, indirect index 136.

And in `exe_sym_addr` for `_mach_init_routine`:
```c
if (err_name && nl_for_elfsym && nl_for_elfsym[symidx] >= 0) {
    ElfW(Sym) *sm = &((ElfW(Sym) *)s1->symtab->data)[symidx];
    if (sm->st_shndx == SHN_UNDEF) {
        target_addr = nlptrs[nl_for_elfsym[symidx]].slot_addr;
        err_name = NULL;
    }
}
```

`target_addr = 0x77154`. Then:
- `R_PPC_ADDR16_HA` of `0x77154`: `imm = (0x77154 + 0x8000) >> 16 = 7`
- `R_PPC_ADDR16_LO` of `0x77154`: `imm = 0x7154`

This gives `lis r2, 0x7; lwz r2, 0x7154(r2)` — EXACTLY what we see!

So the code IS doing the right thing: crt1's `__start` function is loading from `_mach_init_routine`'s nl_symbol_ptr slot. Then `lwz r2, 0(r2)` dereferences that address, expecting `_mach_init_routine` to be a pointer to a function (a function pointer variable), not a function itself.

So `_mach_init_routine` is indeed a `void (*)(void)` pointer variable — `_mach_init_routine` is a function pointer that points to an initialization function. dyld fills the nl_slot with the address of `_mach_init_routine` variable, and crt1 reads through that nl_slot to get the function pointer, then calls through it.
